does: This paper identifies and formalizes a critical vulnerability in Bring-Your-Own-Key agent architectures where a malicious relay can intercept and modify LLM responses after alignment but before agent execution. The authors introduce the Relay Tampering Attack (RTA), which strategically rewrites model outputs across multiple rounds, makes minimal edits to evade detection, and restores stealth by resubmitting tampered outputs to the upstream LLM for validation. They evaluate RTA across multiple benchmarks (AgentDojo and ASB) and six different LLMs, demonstrating up to 99.1% attack success rates while outperforming prompt-injection baselines. The work includes case studies on real-world agent systems like OpenClaw and Claude Code, evaluation of existing defenses showing their inadequacy, and proposes a time-based detection mechanism that reduces vulnerability while maintaining agent functionality.
connects: This work extends the security analysis of aligned language models by shifting focus from training-time alignment to post-generation integrity threats, complementing prior research on prompt injection, jailbreaking, and model safety. It builds on the growing literature examining vulnerabilities in multi-stage AI systems and agent architectures, particularly as deployment patterns increasingly involve third-party infrastructure and relay systems. The paper responds to the implicit assumption in prior alignment work that controlling model outputs at generation time provides sufficient security guarantees, showing instead that end-to-end cryptographic integrity is necessary. It connects to earlier work on adversarial robustness and trojan attacks by demonstrating how intermediate layers in ML pipelines can be exploited, while also engaging with emerging concerns about supply chain security in AI systems.
enables: This work opens several important research directions in AI system security. It motivates the need for end-to-end integrity verification mechanisms in agent architectures, potentially driving adoption of cryptographic signatures and attestation protocols in production LLM systems. The identification of response-path tampering as a distinct threat class enables future work on designing fundamentally more secure agent communication protocols and trusted execution environments for LLM relays. The paper also points toward better detection and monitoring techniques that can identify subtle tampering without computational overhead, and raises questions about the fundamental tension between agent utility and security in untrusted relay environments. Finally, it could spur development of agent architectures that reduce reliance on single-point-of-failure relays or implement decentralized verification schemes for critical decisions.
